docs.title: Titan Production Go-Live Checklist
docs.text: Purpose: Systematic verification before and after arming the production system.

docs.title: Infrastructure
docs.text:
• [ ] Droplet created with correct specs (s-4vcpu-8gb, AMS3)
  • [ ] Cloud Firewall active (only 22/80/443 exposed)
  • [ ] SSH key authentication working
  • [ ] Host bootstrap complete (Docker, UFW, fail2ban)

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
• [ ] DNS A record created for titan.peycheff.com
  • [ ] DNS propagated (dig returns correct IP)
  • [ ] .env.prod populated with production secrets
  • [ ] No CHANGE_ME placeholders remaining
  • [ ] System explicitly DISARMED before deployment (./scripts/ops/set_trading_mode.sh disarm ...)

docs.title: CI/CD
docs.text:
• [ ] GitHub secrets updated:
  • [ ] PROD_SSH_HOST
  • [ ] PROD_SSH_USER
  • [ ] PROD_SSH_KEY
  • [ ] TITAN_RELEASE_KEY

docs.title: Post-Deployment Verification
docs.text: Run: ./scripts/ops/do/04_verify.sh

docs.title: Network
docs.text:
• [ ] DNS resolves titan.peycheff.com to Droplet IP
  • [ ] TLS certificate valid (Let's Encrypt)
  • [ ] HTTP redirects to HTTPS
  • [ ] Console UI accessible at https://titan.peycheff.com/

docs.title: Port Audit (External)
docs.text:
nmap -Pn -p 22,80,443,3000,5432,6379,9090 <DROPLET_IP>
- [ ] Port 22 (SSH): OPEN - [ ] Port 80 (HTTP): OPEN - [ ] Port 443 (HTTPS): OPEN - [ ] Port 3000 (Grafana): FILTERED/CLOSED - [ ] Port 5432 (Postgres): FILTERED/CLOSED - [ ] Port 6379 (Redis): FILTERED/CLOSED - [ ] Port 9090 (Prometheus): FILTERED/CLOSED

docs.title: Services
docs.text:
• [ ] All containers running: docker ps
  • [ ] titan-brain health: OK
  • [ ] titan-execution health: OK
  • [ ] titan-nats health: OK
  • [ ] titan-postgres ready: OK
  • [ ] titan-redis ping: PONG

docs.title: Safety
docs.text:
• [ ] Brain logs show SYSTEM DISARMED BY OPERATOR
  • [ ] Execution logs show EXECUTION DISARMED
  • [ ] No secrets visible in docker logs

docs.title: Arming Procedure (DANGER)
docs.text: [!CAUTION] Only proceed if ALL checklist items above are verified.

docs.title: Step 1: Final Verification
docs.text:
./scripts/ops/do/04_verify.sh
# All checks must pass

docs.title: Step 2: Review Risk Policy
docs.text:
• Confirm risk limits are correctly configured
  • Confirm exchange API keys have appropriate permissions
  • Confirm trading budget limits are set

docs.title: Step 3: Arm System
docs.text:
# Publish canonical ARM action
./scripts/ops/set_trading_mode.sh arm "Go live" "<operator_id>"

# Verify mode change in both services
docker logs titan-brain --tail 100 2>&1 | grep "SYSTEM ARMED BY OPERATOR"
docker logs titan-execution --tail 100 2>&1 | grep "EXECUTION ARMED"

docs.title: Step 4: Monitor First Hour
docs.text:
• Watch logs for errors: docker logs -f titan-brain
  • Monitor execution: docker logs -f titan-execution
  • Check for unexpected trades
  • Verify risk limits are enforced

docs.title: Disarming (Return to Safe Mode)
docs.text:
# Preferred: publish canonical DISARM action
./scripts/ops/set_trading_mode.sh disarm "Manual disarm" "<operator_id>"

# Optional hard-stop if immediate halt required
docker exec titan-nats nats pub titan.cmd.sys.halt.v1 \
  '{"state":"HARD_HALT","reason":"Manual disarm","timestamp":'$(date +%s)'}'

docs.title: Sign-Off
docs.text: Role Name Date Signature Operator Reviewer

docs.title: Production Environment Variables
docs.text:
[!IMPORTANT] This document has been moved to the canonical location: 👉 docs/dev/configuration.md

Please update your bookmarks.

docs.title: Backup & Disaster Recovery
docs.text: Status: Canonical RPO: 1 Hour (Data Loss Tolerance) RTO: 4 Hours (Recovery Time)

docs.title: 1.1 Database (Postgres)
docs.text:
• Schedule: Every 6 hours.
  • Retention: 7 Days (Local), 30 Days (S3 Cold Storage).
  • Content: Full dump of titan_brain.

docs.title: 1.2 Configuration
docs.text:
• Files: .env.prod, config/names.conf, risk_policy.json.
  • Strategy: GitOps. Repository is the backup for config.

docs.title: 2.1 Scenario A: Corrupted Database
docs.text: Symptom: Brain fails to boot due to SQL errors. Procedure: 1. Stop Titan. 2. Run scripts/ops/restore_db.sh latest. 3. Start Titan. 4. Verify allocation_history is intact.

docs.title: 2.2 Scenario B: Droplet Loss (Total Destruction)
docs.text: Symptom: Host unreachable. Procedure: 1. Provision new Droplet (via Terraform/Console). 2. Clone Repo. 3. Deploy Secrets (from offline storage). 4. Run deploy_prod.sh. 5. Restore Database from S3.

docs.title: 3. Restore Drills
docs.text: Frequency: Monthly. Process: restore the Production backup to a Local Dev instance and verify npm run test:brain passes against it.

docs.title: Incident Response Protocol
docs.text: Status: Canonical Policy: Stop the Line

docs.title: 1. Severity Levels
docs.text: Level Definition Response Time Example SEV-1 (CRITICAL) Capital at risk. Trading Halted. Immediate Exchange API disconnect while in position; Truth Drift; Host compromise. SEV-2 (HIGH) Core function degraded. Trading Paused. < 15 mins History sync failing; Strategy latency spiking. SEV-3 (LOW) Non-critical bug. Trading continues. Next day UI glitch; Logging noise.

docs.title: 2.1 The Kill Switch (SEV-1)
docs.text:
ANY Operator is authorized to hit the Kill Switch if they suspect a SEV-1. "Better safe than sorry."

docker exec titan-nats nats pub titan.cmd.risk.halt.v1 '{"reason":"Incident SEV1","auth":"operator"}'

docs.title: 2.2 Investigation
docs.text:
1. Isolate: If it's a security breach, cut network info.
  2. Diagnose: Check titan-brain logs for "PANIC" or "REJECT".
  3. Status Page: Update internal status (Slack/Discord).

docs.title: 3. Post-Incident
docs.text: Every SEV-1 and SEV-2 requires a Post-Mortem within 24 hours.

docs.title: Post-Mortem Template
docs.text:
• Summary: What happened?
  • Timeline: Detection -> Mitigation -> Resolution.
  • Root Cause: The technical "Why".
  • Corrective Actions: (Jira tickets).
    • [ ] Fix the bug.
    • [ ] Add the test case (Regression prevention).
    • [ ] Update documentation.

docs.title: Observability & Metrics
docs.text: Status: Canonical Stack: Prometheus / Grafana / Loki

docs.title: 1. Key Performance Indicators (SLOs)
docs.text: Metric Threshold Consequence Execution Latency < 100ms (p99) Warning Execution Latency > 500ms (p99) SEV-2 (Pause High Freq) Brain Tick Rate > 0.5Hz Warning (Brain is lagging) Error Rate > 1% SEV-2

docs.title: 2.1 "The Cockpit" (Home)
docs.text:
• Equity Curve (Real-time).
  • Active Positions (with PnL).
  • Risk Gauge: Total Exposure vs Limit.
  • Circuit Breaker Status: NORMAL / HALTED.

docs.title: 2.2 "The Engine Room" (Technical)
docs.text:
• NATS Lag: Pending messages in stream.
  • Postgres Connections.
  • Container CPU/RAM.

docs.title: 3. Alerts (Alertmanager)
docs.text:
• Critical: InstanceDown, HighDrift, LowBalance.
  • Warning: HighLatency, MissingHeartbeat.

docs.title: 4. Log Standards
docs.text:
Services must log in JSON format (Production). 

{"level":"info","ts":1700000000,"msg":"order_submitted","order_id":"123"}
Invariant: No PII/Secrets in logs.

docs.title: Operations Runbook
docs.text:
Status: Canonical Authority: Titan Ops Scope: Day-2 Operations (Monitoring, Maintenance, Incident Response)

This document dictates routine and emergency operational procedures for the Titan Trading System.

docs.title: 1. Service Management
docs.text: Target: DigitalOcean Droplet (titan-prod). All commands run from /opt/titan.

docs.title: 1.1 Start / Stop / Restart
docs.text:
# Start Stack (Production)
docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d

# Stop Stack
docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml stop

# Restart Specific Service (e.g., Brain)
docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml restart titan-brain

# Force Recreation (if config changed)
docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d --force-recreate --no-deps titan-brain

docs.title: 1.2 Status Checks
docs.text:
# Docker Status
docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml ps

# NATS Connectivity
docker exec titan-nats nats stream ls

# Database Connectivity
docker exec titan-postgres pg_isready

docs.title: 2.1 Live Logs (Tail)
docs.text:
# Tail all services
docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml logs -f --tail=100

# Focus on Brain (Orchestrator)
docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml logs -f --tail=200 titan-brain

# Focus on Execution (Rust Engine)
docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml logs -f --tail=200 titan-execution

docs.title: 2.2 Metrics
docs.text:
• Prometheus: Internal port 9090.
  • Grafana: Internal port 3000. Access via SSH Tunnel: 
    ssh -L 3000:localhost:3000 deploy@<DROPLET_IP>
# Open http://localhost:3000

docs.title: 2.3 Health Endpoints
docs.text: Service Endpoint Internal URL Note Brain /health http://localhost:3100/health Deep checks DB/NATS Execution /health http://localhost:3002/health Checks internal state NATS /healthz http://localhost:8222/healthz Cluster status

docs.title: 3. Secrets & Rotation
docs.text: Storage: /opt/titan/compose/.env.prod (Owner: root, Mode: 0600).

docs.title: 3.1 Rotate Database Password
docs.text:
1. Stop Services: docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml stop titan-brain titan-execution
  2. Update Postgres:
  3. Edit .env.prod: Update TITAN_DB_PASSWORD.
  4. Start Postgres: docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d titan-postgres
  5. Access container: docker exec -it titan-postgres psql -U titan -d titan_brain
  6. SQL: ALTER USER titan WITH PASSWORD 'new_password';
  7. Restart Services: docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d

docs.title: 3.2 Rotate HMAC Secret (Critical)
docs.text:
Impact: Invalidates all in-flight signatures. Requires simultaneous restart.

  1. Generate new secret: openssl rand -hex 32
  2. Update HMAC_SECRET in .env.prod.
  3. Atomic Restart: 
    docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d --force-recreate titan-brain titan-execution

docs.title: 4.1 Automated Backups
docs.text:
Cron job configured on host at /etc/cron.d/titan-backup: 

0 2 * * * root /opt/titan/scripts/ops/backup_db.sh > /var/log/titan-backup.log 2>&1
Location: /opt/titan/backups/db/

docs.title: 4.2 Manual Backup
docs.text: ./scripts/ops/backup_db.sh verification_snapshot

docs.title: 4.3 Database Restore
docs.text:
Warning: Destructive action. 

# 1. Stop Brain to prevent writes
docker stop titan-brain

# 2. Restore
cat backups/db/titan_brain_2026-02-06.sql | docker exec -i titan-postgres psql -U titan -d titan_brain

# 3. Restart Brain
docker start titan-brain

docs.title: 5.1 Disk Space Cleanup
docs.text:
Run weekly to prevent log explosion. 

docker system prune -f --volumes
# Check usage
df -h

docs.title: 5.2 OS Updates
docs.text:
DigitalOcean managed, but manual check recommended monthly: 

sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade

docs.title: 6. Self-Hosted AI (Kimi-k2.5)
docs.text:
To connect to private GPU infrastructure: 1. Verify GPU Droplet IP. 2. Update .env.prod: 

AI_PROVIDER=kimi-local
KIMI_LOCAL_ENDPOINT=http://<GPU_IP>:8000/v1
3. Restart titan-ai-quant and titan-brain.

docs.title: Production Deployment Runbook
docs.text:
Status: Canonical Authority: Titan Ops Target: DigitalOcean Droplet (titan-prod) Source: github.com/peycheff-com/titan-trading-system

This document is the single source of truth for deploying the Titan Trading System to production. It replaces all legacy deployment guides.

docs.title: 1. The Deployment Contract
docs.text: Deployment is a manual, high-assurance event. It must be performed by an authorized operator. Invariant: Code only flows from main branch to Production.

docs.title: 1.1 Target Environment
docs.text: Component Value Notes Provider DigitalOcean Droplet: s-4vcpu-8gb-pcie (AMS3) Domain titan.peycheff.com SSL via Traefik (Let's Encrypt) SSH User deploy Key-based auth only Path /opt/titan Runtime directory Secrets /opt/titan/compose/.env.prod Protected (0600)

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
• [ ] SSH Access: ssh deploy@<IP> works.
  • [ ] Secrets: /opt/titan/compose/.env.prod exists and is owned by root.
  • [ ] Firewall: UFW allows ONLY ssh (22), http (80), https (443).

docs.title: Application State
docs.text:
• [ ] CI Status: GitHub Actions build for main is 🟢 PASSING.
  • [ ] No formatting/linting errors in the commit to be deployed.
  • [ ] Operator Disarm: If actively trading, issue a DISARM command first (see below).

docs.title: Step 1: Connect and Prepare
docs.text:
ssh deploy@<DROPLET_IP>
cd /opt/titan

docs.title: Step 2: Update Codebase
docs.text:
# Fetch latest changes
git fetch origin main
git reset --hard origin/main

# Verify commit hash
git rev-parse HEAD
# Output MUST match your local CI-passing commit

docs.title: Step 3: Atomic Deployment
docs.text:
We use docker-compose.prod.yml as the definition of reality.

# 1. Pull new images (minimizes downtime)
docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml pull

# 2. Atomic Recreation (Zero-downtime for stateless, Restart for Brain/Exec)
# --remove-orphans ensures no zombie containers remain
docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d --remove-orphans

docs.title: Step 4: Verification (The "Green" Signal)
docs.text:
Wait 30 seconds for specific health checks.

# 1. Check Container Status
docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml ps
# EXPECT: All services 'Up' (brain, execution, nats, postgres, redis, traefik)

# 2. Check Logs for Panic/Errors
docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml logs --tail=50 titan-brain titan-execution
# SEARCH FOR: "Panic", "Error", "Connection refused"

# 3. Verify HTTP Endpoints (Curl)
curl -f https://titan.peycheff.com/health || echo "❌ Console Down"
# Note: Brain/Execution ports are not exposed publically. Check internally if needed:
# docker exec titan-brain curl -f http://localhost:3100/health

docs.title: 4. Rollback Procedure (Emergency)
docs.text: If verification fails or alerts trigger within 5 minutes:

docs.title: Fast Rollback (Previous Code)
docs.text:
# 1. Revert Git
git checkout HEAD@{1}

# 2. Redeploy
docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d --build

docs.title: Nuclear Rollback (Restore from Backup)
docs.text: If data corruption is suspected: 1. Stop services: docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml down 2. Restore Database (see ops/backup_restore_dr.md). 3. Redeploy HEAD@{1}.

docs.title: 5. Policy & Configuration Update Protocol
docs.text:
Critical: If changing risk_policy.json or config/nats.conf:

  1. Disarm Execution: 
    ./scripts/ops/set_trading_mode.sh disarm "Policy Update" "<operator_id>"
  2. Deploy (Steps 1-3 above).
  3. Verify Policy Hash: Check logs of titan-execution to ensure it loaded the exact hash present in titan-brain.
  4. Re-Arm: 
    ./scripts/ops/set_trading_mode.sh arm "Post-Deploy Arm" "<operator_id>"

docs.title: 6. Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• 502 Bad Gateway: Traefik cannot reach the backend. Check titan-console and titan-brain container status.
  • Database Connection Error: Verify TITAN_DB_PASSWORD in .env.prod.
  • NATS Connection Error: Verify NATS_USER/NATS_PASS match in all services.

docs.title: The Brain Decision Loop
docs.text: Status: Canonical Model: Active Inference

docs.title: 1. The Loop
docs.text: The Brain runs a continuous event-driven loop. It does not "poll"; it reacts.

docs.title: Phase 1: Sensing (Input)
docs.text: Brain subscribes to titan.evt.phase.*.signal. - Input: IntentEnvelope<SignalPayload> - Validation: - Is the ts fresh? (< 5s) - Is the source authorized? - Is risk_score within bounds?

docs.title: Phase 2: Perception (State Update)
docs.text: Brain updates its internal "Belief State" (MarketRegime). - Volatility Check: High vol? Shrink allocations. - Budget Check: Do we have free capital in the allocation_history?

docs.title: Phase 3: Action Selection (Policy)
docs.text: If the signal is Accepted: 1. Position Sizing: Applies Kelly Criterion (modified by volatility). 2. Constraints: Checks specific risk_policy.json limits (e.g., Max Open Orders). 3. Signing: - Generates titan.cmd.execution.place.v1 payload. - Signs payload with HMAC_SECRET.

docs.title: Phase 4: Actuation (Output)
docs.text: Brain publishes the command to NATS. It then spawns a "Pending Expectation": - Expectation: "I expect a Fill Event within 2 seconds." - Timeout: If no fill arrives, log TIMEOUT and check system health.

docs.title: 2. Allocation Logic
docs.text:
Brain allocates capital based on specific "Buckets". - Trap Bucket: For Scavenger. Small, fast turnover. - Trend Bucket: For Hunter. Large, slow turnover. - Arb Bucket: For Sentinel. High capacity.

Invariant: Sum(Allocations) <= TotalEquity. Brain never allows leverage to exceed global caps (10x).

docs.title: Execution Engine (Rust)
docs.text: Status: Canonical Stack: Rust, Actix, Redb, Tokio

docs.title: 1. Core Responsibilities
docs.text:
The Execution Engine (titan-execution-rs) is the somatic motor cortex. It moves the money.

  1. Connector: Manages WebSocket/REST connections to Exchanges.
  2. Guardian: Enforces Risk Policy at the point of trade.
  3. Ledger: Records local "Truth" (Shadow State).

docs.title: 2.1 Actor System
docs.text: The engine uses the Actor Model (Tokio channels): - NatsActor: Handles inbound/outbound event bus traffic. - ExchangeActor: Handles specific exchange API mechanics. - RiskActor: Synchronous gatekeeper. The bottleneck by design. - StateActor: Manages Redb reads/writes.

docs.title: 2.2 Persistence (Redb)
docs.text: We use redb (Embedded, ACID, pure Rust) for local state. - Speed: < 100µs writes. - Safety: MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control). - Path: /data/execution.db.

docs.title: 3. The Execution Pipeline
docs.text:
When a Command arrives:

  1. Decoder: Validate JSON and HMAC Signature.
  2. Risk Check:
    • Load account_state (Equity, Positions).
    • RiskGuard::check(cmd, state).
    • If Fail: Respond RejectEvent immediately.
  3. Submission:
    • Serialize to Exchange format (e.g., CCXT-like payload).
    • HTTP POST to Exchange.
  4. Confirmation (EventLoop):
    • Wait for REST response (Order ID).
    • Wait for WS ExecutionReport (Fill Price).
    • Write to Redb.
    • Ack to NATS.

docs.title: 4. Shadow State
docs.text:
Execution maintains a local copy of all Open Orders and Positions. Why? - Speed: Risk checks operate on local RAM, not API calls. - Resilience: If Exchange goes dark, we know what we intend to have.

Reconciliation: The Shadow State is periodically (1m) compared against the Exchange State. Discrepancies trigger a Truth Drift Alert.

docs.title: Organism Phases (Specialized Organs)
docs.text:
Status: Canonical Concept: Division of Labor

The Titan Organism trades through distinct "Phases" or organs. Each Phase has a specific psychological profile, capital mandate, and kill condition.

docs.title: 1. Phase 1: Scavenger (The Trap)
docs.text:
Detailed in services/titan-phase1-scavenger

  • Profile: High frequency, low latency, mean reversion.
  • Microstructure: Order Book Imbalance, Trap detection.
  • Capital: Minimal ($200 - $5,000). High leverage (up to 10x).
  • Goal: "Scavenge" small inefficiencies. High win-rate, small R/R.
  • Kill Condition: consecutive_losses > 5.

docs.title: 2. Phase 2: Hunter (The Eye)
docs.text:
Detailed in services/titan-phase2-hunter

  • Profile: Trend following, Breakout, Holographic Support/Resistance.
  • Microstructure: Volume Profile (VPVR), Fibonacci, Market Structure.
  • Capital: Moderate ($5k - $50k). Medium leverage (3-5x).
  • Goal: Catch the "Meat" of the move. Looser stops, higher R/R.
  • Kill Condition: Volatility < Threshold (Market is dead).

docs.title: 3. Phase 3: Sentinel (The Shield)
docs.text:
Detailed in services/titan-phase3-sentinel

  • Profile: Market Neutral, Basis Arbitrage (Spot vs Perp).
  • Microstructure: Funding Rate disparity.
  • Capital: High ($50k+). Low leverage (1x - 3x).
  • Goal: Yield farming. Fund the other phases.
  • Kill Condition: Funding Rate flips negative.

docs.title: 4. Phase 5: Brain (The Cortex)
docs.text:
Detailed in services/titan-brain

  • Profile: Meta-Strategy.
  • Role:
  • Does not generate signals.
  • Evaluates signals from Phases 1-3.
  • Allocates capital dynamically based on Regime.
  • Example: In "High Volatility Bull", Brain maximizes Phase 2 (Hunter) and minimizes Phase 3 (Sentinel).

docs.title: 5. Phase Contracts (The Interface)
docs.text:
All phase services (Scavenger, Hunter, Sentinel) must implement:

  1. Signal Emission:
    • Subject: titan.evt.{phase}.signal.v1
    • Payload: IntentEnvelope<SignalPayload>
  2. Telemetry:
    • Subject: titan.evt.phase.diagnostics.v1
    • Rate: 1Hz
  3. Command Listener:
    • Subject: titan.cmd.{phase}.config
    • Action: Update internal parameters (e.g., stop loss width) on the fly.

docs.title: Database Reference (PostgreSQL)
docs.text:
Status: Canonical Schema: services/titan-brain/src/db/schema.sql Owner: Titan Brain

The Database (titan_brain) is the persistent memory of the organism.

docs.title: 1.1 Core Tables (The Ledger)
docs.text: Table Description Partitioned? Owner fills Record of every executed trade. YES (Monthly) Execution/Brain event_log Event sourcing journal. YES (Monthly) Brain brain_decisions Why a trade was taken (Audit). No Brain circuit_breaker_events Halt/Resume log. No Brain

docs.title: 1.2 State Tables
docs.text: Table Description allocation_history Time-series of capital allocation per phase. high_watermark Peak equity tracking (for drawdown calc). operators Admin users and permissions. config_history Audit trail of config changes.

docs.title: 2. Partitioning Strategy
docs.text:
High-volume tables are partitioned by time (Monthly) to ensure query performance and efficient archival.

  • Fills: fills_y2026m02, fills_y2026m03...
  • Events: event_log_y2026m02...

Maintenance: Partitions are managed automatically by the pg_partman extension (or manual migration scripts in services/titan-brain/migrations).

docs.title: 3. Row Level Security (RLS)
docs.text: All tables have RLS enabled by default to prevent accidental cross-tenant data leaks (though Titan is currently single-tenant).

docs.title: 4. Migrations
docs.text:
Located in: services/titan-brain/migrations/. Run via: npm run start:brain (Auto-migrates on boot in Dev, validated in Prod).

Invariant: Schema changes must be backward compatible or require a maintenance window.

docs.title: HTTP Interfaces & Ports
docs.text:
Status: Canonical Scope: Internal API Surfaces

Titan is primarily Event-Driven (NATS). HTTP is used for: 1. Health Checks (Orchestration). 2. Metrics (Prometheus). 3. Console BFF (UI Data). 4. Legacy REST adapters (Exchange Proxy).

Invariant: Trading Commands NEVER go over HTTP (except internal adapter calls to exchange). All internal command and control is NATS.

docs.title: NATS JetStream Reference
docs.text: Status: Canonical Authority: @titan/shared Auto-Generated: Partially (via SOTA_QUALITY.md)

docs.title: 1. Subject Taxonomy
docs.text:
Titan uses a strict hierarchical subject space: titan.<type>.<domain>.<action>.<version>.

  • CMD (titan.cmd.>): Commands. Actions that mutate state. 1-to-1 (Queue Group).
  • EVT (titan.evt.>): Events. Facts that happened. 1-to-Many (Broadcast).
  • DATA (titan.data.>): Ephemeral high-frequency data (Tickers).

docs.title: 2. Stream Definitions
docs.text: Stream Description Retention Subjects TITAN_COMMANDS Critical Registry WorkQueue titan.cmd.> TITAN_EVENTS Audit Log Limits (Age/Size) titan.evt.> TITAN_DATA Telemetry Limits (Interest) titan.data.>

docs.title: 3.1 Commands (Critical)
docs.text: Subject Pattern Payload Description titan.cmd.execution.place.v1 IntentEnvelope Brain → Execution. The only way to trade. titan.cmd.risk.halt.v1 HaltPayload Operator → System. Instant Kill Switch. titan.cmd.operator.arm.v1 ArmPayload Operator → System. Re-enable trading. titan.cmd.operator.disarm.v1 DisarmPayload Operator → System. Pause trading safely. titan.cmd.ai.optimize.v1 OptimizePayload Brain → AI Quant. Request new parameters.

docs.title: 3.2 Events (Signals & Fills)
docs.text: Subject Pattern Payload Description titan.evt.execution.fill.v1 FillEvent Execution → Brain. Trade confirmed. titan.evt.execution.reject.v1 RejectEvent Execution → Brain. Order failed validation. titan.evt.scavenger.signal.v1 SignalPayload Scavenger → Brain. Trap detected. titan.evt.hunter.signal.v1 SignalPayload Hunter → Brain. Structure detected. titan.evt.sentinel.signal.v1 SignalPayload Sentinel → Brain. Arb opportunity. titan.evt.phase.diagnostics.v1 PhasePosture All Phases → Brain. Heartbeat/Health.

docs.title: 3.3 Data (Metrics)
docs.text: Subject Pattern Description titan.data.market.ticker.v1 Normalized ticker updates (from Execution/Feed). titan.data.powerlaw.metrics.v1 Real-time Power Law deviation metrics. titan.data.dashboard.update.v1 Aggregated view for Console UI.

docs.title: 4. Message Envelopes
docs.text:
All critical messages (CMD, EVT) must be wrapped in the Intent Envelope.

interface IntentEnvelope<T> {
  id: string;         // UUID v4
  source: string;     // e.g., "titan-brain"
  ts: number;         // Unix ms
  payload: T;         // Similar to Body
  sig?: string;       // HMAC-SHA256 (Required for Commands)
}

docs.title: AI Quant Pipeline
docs.text:
Status: Canonical Engine: Google Gemini 2.0 Flash

Titan uses Generative AI not to trade, but to optimize. The AI is a Researcher, not a Trader.

docs.title: 1. The Loop
docs.text:
1. Data Ingestion: titan-ai-quant ingests 30 days of trade logs (fills table) and market data.
  2. Analysis:
    • It computes "Actionable Discrepancies" (e.g., "Stop losses were too tight in clusters A, B, C").
    • It prompts Gemini 2.0: "Given risk policy X and performance Y, suggest parameter Z modifications."
  3. Proposal:
    • AI generates a ParameterProposal (JSON).
    • Example: hunter.entry_threshold: 0.5 -> 0.55.
  4. Simulation:
    • The Proposal is run through titan-backtesting.
  5. Review:
    • If Backtest > Baseline, likely candidate.
    • Operator Step: Human must review and click "Merge".

docs.title: 2. Safety Invariants
docs.text:
1. No Direct Control: The AI cannot issue titan.cmd.execution.place. It never touches the order book.
  2. Bounded Context: The AI can only modify specific "Tunable Parameters" defined in the strategy schema. It cannot change the Risk Policy.
  3. Deterministic output: We set temperature: 0 for analysis tasks to ensure reproducibility.

docs.title: Research & Reproducibility Standards
docs.text:
Status: Canonical Philosophy: Scientific Skepticism

In Titan, we assume all backtests are overfit until proven otherwise.

docs.title: 1. The Burden of Proof
docs.text:
Any new strategy (Phase) or parameter change (AI Quant) must pass the Triple Gate:

  1. Backtest (Simulation): Does it work on historical data?
  2. Forward Test (Paper): Does it work on live data without money?
  3. Canary Test (Live): Does it work with $100?

docs.title: 2.1 Walk-Forward Optimization
docs.text: Strategies must be trained on Period A and tested on Period B. - Train: Jan - Jun - Test: Jul - Dec - Invariant: Testing on Training data is forbidden.

docs.title: 2.2 Regime Bucketing
docs.text:
Strategies must be tagged with their intended regime. - BULL_TREND - BEAR_TREND - CHOP_SIDEWAYS - HIGH_VOLATILITY

Performance is measured conditioned on the regime. A Trend strategy losing money in Chop is acceptable (if Brain allocates 0 to it).

docs.title: 2.3 Transaction Costs (The Killer)
docs.text: Simulations must include: - Taker Fee: 0.055% (Standard) or 0.02% (VIP). - Slippage: Assumed 5bps + volatility impact. - Latency: Assumed 50ms internal + 50ms network.

docs.title: 3. Rejection Criteria
docs.text: A strategy is REJECTED if: - Sharpe Ratio < 1.0 (Annualized). - Max Drawdown > 20%. - Correlation to BTC Buy & Hold > 0.8 (We seek alpha, not beta).

docs.title: RFC 004: Execution Service High Availability (Hot Standby)
docs.text: Status: Proposed Date: 2026-02-02 Author: Antigravity

docs.title: 1. Problem Statement
docs.text: The titan-execution-rs service is currently a Single Point of Failure (SPOF). If the instance crashes, order management and position tracking stops. Restarting takes time, during which market risk is unmanaged.

docs.title: 2. Proposed Solution: Active-Passive Hot Standby
docs.text: We propose running two instances of titan-execution-rs: 1. Active: Processes orders, manages connections to exchanges, publishes fills. 2. Standby: Subscribes to the same inputs but DOES NOT send orders to exchanges. It maintains local state (Shadow State) in sync with the Active instance via NATS events.

docs.title: 3.1 Startup Configuration
docs.text: Both instances start with the same configuration but different identity tags (e.g., primary vs backup). A new flag --active-standby or environment variable EXECUTION_MODE=active|standby will be introduced.

docs.title: 3.2 Leadership Election (Future Scope)
docs.text: Ideally, we use NATS JetStream KeyValue (KV) for leadership election. - KV_ELECTION: { key: "leader", value: "<instance_id>", lease: 5s } - If leader lease expires, Standby promotes itself to Active.

docs.title: 3.3 State Synchronization
docs.text:
• Inputs: Both subscribe to titan.cmd.exec.place.v1.>.
  • Outputs: only Active publishes to titan.evt.exec.**.
  • Standby Behavior:
    • Ingests titan.cmd.exec.place... (updates internal intent state).
    • Ingests titan.evt.exec.fill... (updates internal position state).
    • If promoted, it already has the latest state.

docs.title: 4. Implementation Phase 1: The Flag
docs.text:
Add a startup flag/config to control the behavior.

struct Config {
    // ...
    /// If true, runs in hot-standby mode (no exchange connectivity)
    pub hot_standby: bool,
}

docs.title: 5. Risks
docs.text:
• Split Brain: Both think they are active. Mitigation: NATS KV atomic logic.
  • State Drift: Standby misses a fill. Mitigation: Periodic state reconciliation snapshots.

docs.title: 6. Action Plan
docs.text:
1. Add active_standby configuration field to titan-execution-rs.
  2. Implement logic to suppress exchange writes when hot_standby is true.
  3. Deploy 2nd instance in docker-compose.prod.yml.

docs.title: Circuit Breakers & Kill Switches
docs.text: Status: Canonical Mechanism: State Machine in titan-execution-rs

docs.title: 1. Risk States
docs.text:
The system operates in one of 4 states. Transitions are one-way (downward) during a trading session, requiring operator intervention to reset (upward).

State Trigger Effect 🟢 NORMAL Default Full limits apply. 🟡 CAUTIOUS Drawdown > 50% of Limit Position Sizing = 0.5x. 🟠 DEFENSIVE Drawdown > 75% of Limit No New Positions. Reduce Only. 🔴 EMERGENCY Drawdown > 99% OR HALT Cmd Flatten All. Cancel All.

docs.title: 2.1 Daily Drawdown
docs.text:
• Monitored by Brain.
  • If Equity drops below StartingEquity - MaxDailyLoss, a CMD.RISK.HALT is fired.

docs.title: 2.2 Truth Collapse (Drift)
docs.text:
• Monitored by Reconciliation Loop.
  • If |LocalPos - RemotePos| > Tolerance, the system enters EMERGENCY.

docs.title: 2.3 Sentinel Failure
docs.text: • If titan-phase3-sentinel stops emitting heartbeats (> 10s), Brain triggers DEFENSIVE.

docs.title: 3.1 The Big Red Button (HALT)
docs.text:
Manual override to kill the system. 

# Via NATS (from Host)
docker exec titan-nats nats pub titan.cmd.sys.halt.v1 '{"state":"HARD_HALT","reason":"Manual","timestamp":12345}'
Effect: - Cancels all open orders. - Closes all positions (Market). - Rejects all future commands.

docs.title: 3.2 Reset / Re-Arm
docs.text:
To return to NORMAL state, an Operator must sign an Arm command.

./scripts/ops/set_trading_mode.sh arm "Post-incident re-arm" "<operator_id>"
Prerequisite: - Root cause resolved. - Operator manually reviewed logs.

docs.title: Titan Risk Policy
docs.text: Status: Canonical Source Definitions: packages/shared/risk_policy.json Enforcement: Brain (Strategic) & Execution (Transactional)

docs.title: 1. The Constitution
docs.text:
The Risk Policy is the immutable constitution of the organism. It defines the boundaries that cannot be crossed, regardless of profit potential.

Invariant: The Policy Hash (policy_hash) must match between Brain and Execution at handshake. If it differs, the system Halts.

docs.title: 2. Global Limits
docs.text: Parameter Value Meaning Max Account Leverage 10.0x Hard cap on total notion / total equity. Max Position Notional $50,000 Max size of any single position. Max Daily Loss -$1,000 Kill switch trigger level. Max Open Orders 5 per Symbol. Prevents order spam/runaway. Max Slippage 100 bps (1%) Execution bounds for market orders.

docs.title: Layer 1: Strategic (Brain)
docs.text:
• Allocation: Brain will not allocate capital to a Phase if the budget is exhausted.
  • Correlation: Brain checks portfolio correlation. If Corr(BTC, ETH) > 0.7, it imposes a correlationPenalty (0.5x sizing).

docs.title: Layer 2: Transactional (Execution RiskGuard)
docs.text: Situated in Rust services/titan-execution-rs/src/risk_guard.rs. - Pre-Flight Check: Every command is simulated against the "Shadow State". - Rejection: If NewLeverage > MaxLeverage, the command is rejected with RISK_VIOLATION.

docs.title: Layer 3: Reflexive (Circuit Breakers)
docs.text: See Circuit Breakers.

docs.title: 4. Policy Update Protocol
docs.text:
Changing the policy is a Nuclear Operation.

  1. Edit: Modify packages/shared/risk_policy.json on main.
  2. Deploy:
  3. Both Brain and Execution must be redeployed.
  4. They will compute the new SHA256 hash on boot.
  5. Handshake will verify the new hash match.
  6. Audit: The change is logged in the config_history table.

docs.title: 5. Approved Instruments
docs.text:
Whitelist Only: - BTC/USDT - ETH/USDT - SOL/USDT

Trading any other symbol results in immediate command rejection.

docs.title: PowerLaw Mode Runbooks
docs.text: Operational procedures for Titan's Power Law tail-risk system.

docs.title: Mode Overview
docs.text: Mode Constraints Published Brain Impact Execution Engine SHADOW Yes (no effect) Logs only No enforcement ADVISORY Yes (alerts) Alerts to console Soft limits, warnings ENFORCEMENT Yes (enforced) Active alerts Hard limits, blocks trades

docs.title: SHADOW Mode
docs.text: Purpose: Observe PowerLaw metrics without affecting trading.

docs.title: Activation
docs.text:
# Via environment
POWERLAW_MODE=shadow

# Via Brain API
curl -X POST http://brain:8080/api/powerlaw/mode \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"mode": "SHADOW"}'

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
• Check metrics at /metrics endpoint
  • Watch titan_powerlaw_tail_alpha gauge
  • Review logs for constraint computation

docs.title: Expected Behavior
docs.text:
• Constraints published to NATS
  • No trading impact
  • Console shows real-time metrics
  • Execution engine ignores constraints

docs.title: ADVISORY Mode
docs.text: Purpose: Alert operators to tail-risk conditions without blocking trades.

docs.title: Activation
docs.text: POWERLAW_MODE=advisory

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
• Console alerts for high-risk conditions
  • Prometheus alerting on health_status < 1
  • Impact events in titan.evt.powerlaw.impact.v1

docs.title: Expected Behavior
docs.text:
• Soft constraints computed
  • Warnings in execution engine logs
  • Orders not blocked (logged only)
  • Impact events record decisions

docs.title: ENFORCEMENT Mode
docs.text: Purpose: Actively enforce tail-risk constraints on order flow.

docs.title: Activation
docs.text: POWERLAW_MODE=enforcement

docs.title: Pre-Flight Checks
docs.text:
1. Verify metrics computation is stable (α in expected range)
  2. Confirm TTL settings (ttl_ms default: 120000)
  3. Check execution engine constraint store is receiving updates
  4. Review recent impact events for expected patterns

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
• titan_powerlaw_constraints_published_total counter
  • Execution engine logs for blocked/modified orders
  • Console shows active constraints per symbol

docs.title: Expected Behavior
docs.text:
• Hard constraints enforced
  • Orders exceeding limits rejected
  • reduce_only blocks new positions when triggered
  • Impact events log all enforcement actions

docs.title: Disable PowerLaw Enforcement
docs.text:
# Immediate disable via env
POWERLAW_MODE=shadow

# Or via API
curl -X POST http://brain:8080/api/powerlaw/mode \
  -d '{"mode": "SHADOW"}'

docs.title: Stale Constraints
docs.text: If constraints become stale (ttl_ms expired): - Execution engine falls back to defensive defaults - reduce_only: true activated - Max notional reduced to 100_000

docs.title: Metric Health Degradation
docs.text: Status Action ok Normal operation stale Investigate data pipeline low_sample Check market data feed fit_failed Review estimator logs

docs.title: Grafana Dashboard Queries
docs.text:
# Tail Alpha per Symbol
titan_powerlaw_tail_alpha{venue="binance"}

# Health Status Distribution
count by (status) (titan_powerlaw_health_status)

# Constraints Published Rate
rate(titan_powerlaw_constraints_published_total[5m])

# Computation Latency
histogram_quantile(0.95, titan_powerlaw_computation_duration_ms_bucket)

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Market data feeds stop updating
  • Order placement fails
  • WebSocket connections drop

docs.title: Immediate Actions
docs.text:
1. Check exchange status page (Binance, Bybit, etc.)
  2. Check Hunter service logs: docker logs titan-phase2-hunter --tail 100
  3. Verify API credentials are valid

docs.title: Recovery
docs.text:
1. If exchange-side: Wait for exchange recovery, services should auto-reconnect
  2. If credential issue: Rotate credentials and restart affected service
  3. If network issue: Check firewall/proxy settings

docs.title: Fallback
docs.text:
• Enable graceful degradation mode if available
  • Halt new order placement until connectivity restored

docs.title: Incident Response Runbook
docs.text: [!IMPORTANT] This runbook defines the authoritative procedures for handling SEV-1 (Critical) and SEV-2 (Major) incidents. All commands assume access to the production NATS bus or CLI tools.

docs.title: Severity Levels
docs.text: Level Description Example Response Time SEV-1 (Critical) System HALTED, Capital at Risk, Security Breach Unsigned commands detected, Position limits breached, Sentinel crash IMMEDIATE (< 5 min) SEV-2 (Major) Degradation of critical path, no immediate capital risk Latency spike > 100ms, One exchange disconnected URGENT (< 30 min) SEV-3 (Minor) Non-critical issues, tooling, logging Metrics delayed, dev environment down Standard (< 4 hours)

docs.title: 1. EMERGENCY KILL SWITCH (The "Oh Shit" Button)
docs.text:
When to use: Market anomaly, runaway algo, potential compromise, or unverified behavior.

Procedure:

  1. Execute Halt (System-Wide): Publish the system halt command via NATS CLI. 

    nats pub "titan.cmd.sys.halt.v1" '{"reason": "MANUAL_INTERVENTION", "operator": "human"}'

  2. Verify Halt:

    • Console: Check header is RED (System Halted).
    • Logs: Verify titan-execution logs show: Order Rejected: System Halted. 
      docker logs titan-execution-rs --tail 50 | grep "System Halted"

  3. Flatten Positions (Optional): > [!WARNING] > Only execute if market conditions allow liquidating all positions. 

    nats pub "titan.cmd.risk.flatten" '{"symbol": "ALL", "reason": "EMERGENCY_FLATTEN"}'

  4. Isolate Input: Stop strategy services to prevent new signal generation. 

    docker compose stop titan-phase1-scavenger titan-phase2-hunter titan-phase3-sentinel

docs.title: 2. Data Corruption / State Recovery
docs.text:
When to use: ShadowState mismatch, duplicate fills, or invalid sequence numbers.

Procedure:

  1. Halt System (See Step 1).
  2. Snapshot Truth (DB): 
    pg_dump -U postgres -h localhost -d titan_prod > dump_$(date +%s).sql
  3. Reset Execution State: If JetStream state is corrupt, delete the consumer to force a replay or fresh start (caution: may replay old messages if not carefully managed). 
    # Delete the persistent consumer
nats consumer delete TITAN_EXECUTION execution_group
  4. Restart Execution Service: 
    docker compose restart titan-execution-rs
  5. Re-Verify State:
    • Compare titan-console positions with Exchange GUI.
    • If mismatch persists, manually align DB state to Exchange truth.

docs.title: 3. Security Breach (Unsigned/Invalid Commands)
docs.text:
When to use: Alert Security: Unsigned Command Detected or HMAC Verification Failed.

Procedure:

  1. Rotate Secrets Immediately:
    • Generate new TITAN_HMAC_SECRET.
    • Update .env.prod (or Vault).
  2. Rolling Restart: 
    docker compose restart titan-brain titan-execution-rs
  3. Forensics:
    • Isolate logs for the actor ID.
    • Revoke Exchange API keys if compromise is suspected (See Secrets Management ).

docs.title: A. Deployment Rollback
docs.text:
When to use: New deployment fails health check or verification.

Procedure: 1. Check Last Good State: 

cat /opt/titan/state/last_known_good.json
2. Execute Rollback Script: 
/opt/titan/scripts/rollback.sh
3. Verify: 
/opt/titan/scripts/verify.sh

docs.title: B. Database Migration Failure
docs.text:
When to use: sota:db fails in prod.

Procedure: 1. Rollback Migration: 

npm run db:down
2. Restore Backup (if data loss occurred): Use the most recent dump_*.sql.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Services fail to publish/consume messages
  • Health checks fail on NATS connectivity
  • Logs show "connection refused" or "timeout" errors

docs.title: Immediate Actions
docs.text:
1. Check NATS container status: docker ps | grep nats
  2. Check NATS logs: docker logs titan-nats --tail 100
  3. Verify network connectivity: nc -zv localhost 4222

docs.title: Recovery
docs.text:
1. Restart NATS: docker restart titan-nats
  2. If persistent, check volume health: docker volume inspect nats_data
  3. Rollback to last known good config if config change caused issue

docs.title: Escalation
docs.text: • If recovery fails after 15 minutes, escalate to on-call lead

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Services fail to query/write to database
  • Health checks fail on DB connectivity
  • Logs show "connection refused" or "too many connections"

docs.title: Immediate Actions
docs.text:
1. Check Postgres container: docker ps | grep postgres
  2. Check Postgres logs: docker logs titan-postgres --tail 100
  3. Verify connections: docker exec titan-postgres psql -U postgres -c "SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity;"

docs.title: Recovery
docs.text:
1. Restart Postgres: docker restart titan-postgres
  2. If connection exhaustion, kill idle connections
  3. If data corruption suspected, restore from backup (see restore_drill)

docs.title: Escalation
docs.text: • Data loss suspected: Immediately escalate to on-call lead

docs.title: Pre-Rollback Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Identify the bad commit/version
  • [ ] Confirm rollback target version is known good
  • [ ] Notify stakeholders

docs.title: 1. Stop Current Services
docs.text: docker compose down

docs.title: 2. Checkout Previous Version
docs.text: git checkout <previous-good-sha>

docs.title: 3. Rebuild and Deploy
docs.text:
make docker-build
docker compose up -d

docs.title: 4. Verify Health
docs.text: curl localhost:3100/health

docs.title: Post-Rollback
docs.text:
• Document incident
  • Create hotfix branch for proper fix
  • Schedule post-mortem

docs.title: Authorization & ACLs
docs.text: Status: Canonical Mechanism: NATS 2.0 Auth (config/nats.conf)

docs.title: 1. Principle of Least Privilege
docs.text: Service identities are isolated. A compromised "Strategy" container cannot empty the wallet because it lacks the Permission to speak to the Execution Engine.

docs.title: 2. NATS Users
docs.text: User Config Name Capabilities Rationale Brain brain PUB: >, SUB: > The Cortex needs to see and say everything. Execution execution PUB: titan.evt.execution.>SUB: titan.cmd.execution.> Can only report fills; cannot order itself to trade. Strategies scavenger, hunter PUB: titan.evt.{phase}.signal Can only speak when they see something. Console console SUB: titan.data.>, titan.evt.> Read-only visibility (mostly).

docs.title: 3. Web UI Authorization
docs.text:
The Console (titan-console) is protected by a 2-layer auth system: 1. Traefik Basic Auth (Optional/Legacy): Edge protection. 2. App Level (JWT): - User logs in with TITAN_MASTER_PASSWORD. - Server issues HttpOnly cookie. - All API routes verify JWT signature (JWT_SECRET).

Invariant: There is no "Guest" mode. You are either the Operator (God Mode) or nothing.

docs.title: Strategy
docs.text:
• Local development: .env files (never commit real secrets).
  • Production: Docker secrets or a dedicated secrets manager (Vault/KMS).
  • Services support *_FILE environment variables to read secrets from mounted files.

docs.title: Docker Secrets (Recommended for Single-Host VPS)
docs.text:
1. Create secret files under ./secrets/ (not committed):
secrets/
  titan_db_password
  titan_hmac_secret
  binance_api_key
  binance_api_secret
  bybit_api_key
  bybit_api_secret
  gemini_api_key
  grafana_admin_password
  1. Deploy with the secrets overlay:
docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml -f docker-compose.secrets.yml up -d
  1. Services will load the *_FILE values into environment variables at runtime.

docs.title: Vault / KMS (Recommended for Multi-Node)
docs.text:
• Use Vault Agent or KMS sidecar to render secrets to files.
  • Mount rendered files to the containers and set *_FILE variables.
  • Rotate secrets centrally and reload services on rotation.

docs.title: 1. Rotating HMAC Secrets (Zero Downtime)
docs.text:
Titan supports multiple valid HMAC secrets during a transition period.

Phase 1: Add New Secret 1. Generate new secret: openssl rand -hex 32 2. Update configuration (e.g., Vault, K8s Secret): - TITAN_HMAC_SECRET_PRIMARY=<new_secret> - TITAN_HMAC_SECRET_SECONDARY=<old_secret> - TITAN_HMAC_KEY_ID=<new_key_id> 3. Deploy changes to listeners (Execution, Sentinel) first. - Services will accept signatures from both Primary and Secondary secrets. 4. Deploy changes to signers (Brain) last. - Brain will start signing with the new Primary secret.

Phase 2: Deprecate Old Secret 1. Wait 24 hours to ensure all in-flight messages are processed. 2. Remove TITAN_HMAC_SECRET_SECONDARY. 3. Redeploy all services.

docs.title: 2. Rotating Database/NATS Credentials (Rolling Restart)
docs.text:
1. Provision new credentials in the infrastructure (AWS RDS, NATS Server).
  2. Update *_FILE secrets (e.g., /run/secrets/db_password).
  3. If using K8s/Docker Swarm, update the Secret object.
  4. Perform a rolling restart of all services.
  5. titan-brain, titan-execution-rs, etc.
  6. Services will pick up the new file content on startup via loadSecretsFromFiles().

docs.title: 3. Rotating Exchange API Keys
docs.text:
1. Generate new API keys on the Exchange Dashboard.
  2. Update the secret store (Vault/Secrets Manager).
  3. Restart titan-execution-rs and titan-ai-quant (or trigger hot-reload if supported).
  4. Verify connectivity:
  5. Check logs for "Exchange connected".
  6. Place a test order (min size) in Lab Mode.
  7. Revoke old keys on the Exchange.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
• Logs: Ensure no secrets appear in logs. Search for [MASKED].
  • Metrics: Monitor auth_failures metric during rotation.

docs.title: Secrets & Rotation Architecture
docs.text: Status: Canonical Implementation: AES-256-GCM / Docker Secrets

docs.title: 1. Secret Classification
docs.text: Class Examples Storage Rotation S0 (Root) TITAN_MASTER_PASSWORD, SSH Keys Offline / Stick Annually S1 (Infra) POSTGRES_PASSWORD, NATS_SYS_PASS .env.prod / Vault Quarterly S2 (App) HMAC_SECRET, JWT_SECRET .env.prod On Compromise S3 (External) BYBIT_API_KEY, OPENAI_KEY Exchange / .env 90 Days

docs.title: 2. SOTA Credential Management (2026)
docs.text: Titan implements "Zero-Secret" operations where possible. - Frontend: No secrets stored in localstorage. JWT only (HttpOnly). - Backend: Encrypted Vault (AES-256-GCM) for storing Exchange Keys. - IPC: HMAC signatures verifying origin, not just identity.

docs.title: 2.1 The Vault
docs.text: The Config Service (@titan/shared) offers a lightweight Vault abstraction. - On Boot: Loads .env. - On Request: Decrypts values in memory. - Invariant: Never log raw secrets.

docs.title: 3.1 HMAC Rotation (The "Big One")
docs.text: Updating HMAC_SECRET breaks all IPC. Procedure: 1. Stop titan-brain and titan-execution. 2. Update secret in environment. 3. Start both simultaneously.

docs.title: 3.2 Key Revocation
docs.text: If an API key leaks: 1. Kill Switch: CMD.RISK.HALT (Pauses trading). 2. Revoke: Delete key on Exchange website. 3. Rotate: Update .env.prod. 4. Resume: CMD.OPERATOR.ARM.

docs.title: Titan Threat Model
docs.text: Status: Canonical Focus: Adversarial Risk Analysis

docs.title: 1.1 Critical Assets
docs.text:
1. Capital (Exchange Balances): The primary target.
  2. Private Keys (API Keys, SSH): Access to capital.
  3. Strategy IP: The logic and signals (Alpha).
  4. Reputation: The "Identity" of the organism.

docs.title: 1.2 Adversaries
docs.text:
1. External Hackers: Seeking to steal API keys or manipulate orders.
  2. Malicious Insiders: Operators with excess privilege.
  3. The Exchange itself: Seeking to liquidate positions (Adversarial venue).
  4. The System itself: Bugs leading to fast liquidation (The "Drift" enemy).

docs.title: 2.1 Network (Ingress)
docs.text:
• Vectors: SSH Port (22), HTTPS (443).
  • Mitigation:
  • DigitalOcean Cloud Firewall (Whitelisted IPs only for SSH).
  • Traefik Reverse Proxy for HTTP.
  • Invariant: Brain/Execution ports (3100/3002) are NEVER exposed publicly.

docs.title: 2.2 Supply Chain
docs.text:
• Vectors: NIST Vulnerabilities in dependencies, malicious NPM packages.
  • Mitigation:
  • knip analysis in CI.
  • Locked package-lock.json.
  • Minimal container images (Alpine).

docs.title: 2.3 Signal Injection
docs.text:
• Vectors: Injecting fake signals to trigger bad trades.
  • Mitigation:
  • HMAC Signatures: Brain execution commands MUST be signed.
  • Timestamps: Replay protection (max 5s drift).
  • NATS ACLs: scavenger CANNOT publish to titan.cmd.execution.*.

docs.title: 3.1 The "Fail-Closed" Principle
docs.text: If a security check fails (e.g., missing signature, weird timestamp), the system Halts. It never "guesses" or "allows".

docs.title: 3.2 HMAC Signing Layer
docs.text: All Inter-Process Communication (IPC) that carries financial consequence is signed with HMAC-SHA256. - Key: HMAC_SECRET (Env var). - Enforcement: Wiring check at startup. Panic if missing.

docs.title: 3.3 NATS Access Control Lists (ACL)
docs.text: We do not use a flat namespace. - User brain: Full Access. - User execution: Can Publish Fills, Subscribe Commands. CANNOT Publish Commands. - User scavenger: Can Publish Signals. CANNOT Publish Commands.

docs.title: 4. Residual Risks
docs.text:
1. Exchange Account Compromise: If API keys leak, we rely on Exchange-side IP whitelisting.
  2. Social Engineering: Phishing operator for SSH keys. (Mitigation: YubiKey/Hardware MFA for SSH).

docs.title: Titan Infrastructure Setup Guide
docs.text: This guide provides comprehensive instructions for provisioning and configuring the infrastructure required for the Titan Trading System production deployment.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Titan Trading System requires a robust infrastructure foundation with specific minimum requirements to ensure reliable, high-frequency trading operations. This setup includes:

  • VPS instance with minimum specifications (8GB RAM, 4 CPU cores)
  • Base dependencies (Node.js v18+, Redis, PM2, Nginx)
  • Security hardening (firewall, fail2ban, SSL certificates)
  • System optimization for high-frequency trading
  • Automated security updates

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Ubuntu 22.04 LTS server (recommended)
  • Root or sudo access
  • Domain name (optional, for SSL certificates)
  • Email address (for SSL certificate registration)

docs.title: 1. Basic Infrastructure Provisioning
docs.text:
# Clone the repository and navigate to scripts
cd titan-production-deployment
chmod +x scripts/provision-infrastructure.sh

# Run basic provisioning
./scripts/provision-infrastructure.sh

# Or with SSL setup (recommended)
./scripts/provision-infrastructure.sh -d yourdomain.com -e admin@yourdomain.com

docs.title: 2. Validate Installation
docs.text:
Manually verify service status:

docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml ps

docs.title: 3. Configure Environment
docs.text:
# Copy and customize environment configuration
cp config/deployment/infrastructure.env.example config/deployment/infrastructure.env
nano config/deployment/infrastructure.env

docs.title: System Requirements Validation
docs.text:
The infrastructure must meet these minimum requirements:

Component Minimum Requirement Recommended RAM 8GB 16GB+ CPU Cores 4 8+ Disk Space 50GB free 100GB+ SSD Network 100 Mbps 1 Gbps OS Ubuntu 22.04 LTS Ubuntu 22.04 LTS

docs.title: Node.js v18+
docs.text:
• Installed via NodeSource repository
  • Includes npm package manager
  • Global PM2 installation for process management

docs.title: Redis
docs.text:
• Latest stable version
  • Configured for production use
  • Memory limit: 2GB with LRU eviction policy
  • Bound to localhost (127.0.0.1) for security

docs.title: PM2 Process Manager
docs.text:
• Global installation via npm
  • Configured for automatic startup
  • Process monitoring and auto-restart capabilities

docs.title: Nginx Web Server
docs.text:
• Latest stable version
  • Configured as reverse proxy
  • SSL/TLS termination support
  • Gzip compression enabled

docs.title: UFW Firewall
docs.text:
# Default policies
ufw default deny incoming
ufw default allow outgoing

# Allowed ports
ufw allow 22/tcp    # SSH
ufw allow 80/tcp    # HTTP
ufw allow 443/tcp   # HTTPS

# Restricted ports
ufw allow from 127.0.0.1 to any port 6379  # Redis localhost only

docs.title: fail2ban Protection
docs.text:
• SSH brute force protection (3 attempts, 1-hour ban)
  • Nginx authentication failure protection
  • Custom jail configurations for trading system

docs.title: SSL Certificates
docs.text:
• Let's Encrypt certificates via Certbot
  • Automatic renewal via systemd timer
  • Strong cipher suites and protocols

docs.title: Automatic Security Updates
docs.text:
• Unattended upgrades for security patches
  • Configurable reboot policies
  • Email notifications for critical updates

docs.title: File Descriptor Limits
docs.text:
# /etc/security/limits.conf
titan soft nofile 65536
titan hard nofile 65536
titan soft nproc 32768
titan hard nproc 32768

# /etc/sysctl.conf
net.core.rmem_max = 16777216
net.core.wmem_max = 16777216
net.ipv4.tcp_rmem = 4096 87380 16777216
net.ipv4.tcp_wmem = 4096 65536 16777216
net.core.netdev_max_backlog = 5000
vm.swappiness = 10

docs.text:
• config/infrastructure.config.json - Main infrastructure configuration
  • config/deployment/infrastructure.env - Environment-specific settings

• /etc/fail2ban/jail.local - fail2ban jail configuration
  • /etc/ufw/applications.d/titan - UFW application profile
  • /etc/nginx/sites-available/titan - Nginx virtual host configuration

docs.text:
Ensure all containers are running and healthy:

docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml ps

The output should show healthy status for core services (nats, redis, postgres).

docs.title: Redis Security Guide for Titan Trading System
Overview
docs.text: This guide provides comprehensive instructions for securing Redis in the Titan trading system production environment. Redis stores critical trading data and system state, making its security paramount.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
• Password Protection: Redis must be configured with a strong password
  • Password Rotation: Passwords should be rotated every 90 days
  • Environment Variables: Passwords must be stored in environment variables, never in code

docs.title: Network Security
docs.text:
• Bind Address: Redis should only bind to localhost (127.0.0.1)
  • Protected Mode: Always enable protected mode
  • Firewall: Block Redis port (6379) from external access

docs.title: Command Security
docs.text:
• Dangerous Commands: Disable or rename dangerous commands
  • Command Renaming: Rename administrative commands to prevent unauthorized access

docs.title: Automatic Setup (Recommended)
docs.text:
# Run the automated setup script
sudo ./scripts/setup-redis-secure.sh

# This will:
# 1. Generate a secure password
# 2. Configure Redis with secure settings
# 3. Save password to .env file
# 4. Restart Redis service
# 5. Test the configuration

docs.title: Manual Setup
docs.text:
If you prefer manual configuration:

# 1. Generate a secure password
REDIS_PASSWORD=$(openssl rand -base64 32 | tr -d "=+/" | cut -c1-32)

# 2. Configure Redis
sudo ./scripts/setup-redis-secure.sh --password "$REDIS_PASSWORD"

# 3. Test the configuration
sudo ./scripts/setup-redis-secure.sh --test-only --password "$REDIS_PASSWORD"

docs.title: Redis Configuration File
docs.text:
The secure Redis configuration (config/redis-secure.conf) includes:

# Authentication
requirepass ${REDIS_PASSWORD}

# Network Security
bind 127.0.0.1 ::1
protected-mode yes
port 6379

# Disable Dangerous Commands
rename-command FLUSHDB ""
rename-command FLUSHALL ""
rename-command KEYS ""
rename-command CONFIG "CONFIG_b840fc02d524045429941cc15f59e41cb7be6c52"
rename-command SHUTDOWN "SHUTDOWN_b840fc02d524045429941cc15f59e41cb7be6c52"
rename-command DEBUG ""
rename-command EVAL ""

# Memory and Performance
maxmemory 2gb
maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru

# Persistence
save 900 1
save 300 10
save 60 10000
appendonly yes

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Store Redis credentials in .env file:

# Redis Configuration
REDIS_PASSWORD=your_secure_password_here
REDIS_HOST=localhost
REDIS_PORT=6379
REDIS_DB=0

Important: Never commit the .env file to version control!

docs.title: Node.js Connection
docs.text:
const redis = require('redis');

const client = redis.createClient({
  host: process.env.REDIS_HOST || 'localhost',
  port: process.env.REDIS_PORT || 6379,
  password: process.env.REDIS_PASSWORD,
  db: process.env.REDIS_DB || 0,
  retry_strategy: (options) => {
    if (options.error && options.error.code === 'ECONNREFUSED') {
      return new Error('Redis server connection refused');
    }
    if (options.total_retry_time > 1000 * 60 * 60) {
      return new Error('Redis retry time exhausted');
    }
    if (options.attempt > 10) {
      return undefined;
    }
    return Math.min(options.attempt * 100, 3000);
  }
});

client.on('error', (err) => {
  console.error('Redis Client Error:', err);
});

client.on('connect', () => {
  console.log('Connected to Redis');
});

docs.title: Connection String Format
docs.text:
redis://localhost:6379
redis://:password@localhost:6379
redis://:password@localhost:6379/0

docs.title: Password Management
docs.text:
1. Strong Passwords: Use passwords with at least 32 characters
  2. Random Generation: Use cryptographically secure random generation
  3. Regular Rotation: Rotate passwords every 90 days
  4. Secure Storage: Store passwords in environment variables or secure vaults

docs.title: Network Security
docs.text:
1. Localhost Only: Bind Redis to localhost only
  2. Firewall Rules: Block external access to Redis port
  3. VPN Access: Use VPN for remote administration
  4. SSL/TLS: Consider Redis with SSL/TLS for additional security

docs.title: Monitoring and Auditing
docs.text:
1. Connection Monitoring: Monitor Redis connections
  2. Command Auditing: Log administrative commands
  3. Performance Monitoring: Monitor Redis performance metrics
  4. Security Alerts: Set up alerts for authentication failures

docs.title: Backup Security
docs.text:
# Secure Redis backup
redis-cli -a "$REDIS_PASSWORD" --rdb /backups/redis/dump-$(date +%Y%m%d).rdb

# Encrypt backup
gpg --symmetric --cipher-algo AES256 /backups/redis/dump-$(date +%Y%m%d).rdb

docs.title: Recovery with Authentication
docs.text:
The disaster recovery system automatically handles Redis authentication:

# Recovery steps include authentication
{
  "id": "restore-redis-data",
  "command": "redis-cli -a ${REDIS_PASSWORD} --rdb /var/lib/redis/dump.rdb",
  "environment": {
    "REDIS_PASSWORD": "${REDIS_PASSWORD}"
  }
}

docs.title: Connection Testing
docs.text:
# Test Redis connection
redis-cli -a "$REDIS_PASSWORD" ping

# Test authentication requirement
redis-cli ping  # Should return NOAUTH error

# Test basic operations
redis-cli -a "$REDIS_PASSWORD" set test_key "test_value"
redis-cli -a "$REDIS_PASSWORD" get test_key
redis-cli -a "$REDIS_PASSWORD" del test_key

docs.title: Security Validation
docs.text:
# Check Redis configuration
redis-cli -a "$REDIS_PASSWORD" CONFIG GET requirepass
redis-cli -a "$REDIS_PASSWORD" CONFIG GET bind

# Verify dangerous commands are disabled
redis-cli -a "$REDIS_PASSWORD" FLUSHDB  # Should return error
redis-cli -a "$REDIS_PASSWORD" KEYS "*"  # Should return error

docs.title: Performance Testing
docs.text:
# Redis benchmark with authentication
redis-benchmark -a "$REDIS_PASSWORD" -n 10000 -c 10

# Memory usage check
redis-cli -a "$REDIS_PASSWORD" INFO memory

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
1. Authentication Failures 

    # Check password in .env file
grep REDIS_PASSWORD .env

# Test with correct password
redis-cli -a "$(grep REDIS_PASSWORD .env | cut -d'=' -f2)" ping

  2. Connection Refused 

    # Check if Redis is running
sudo systemctl status redis

# Check Redis logs
sudo tail -f /var/log/redis/redis-server.log

  3. Permission Denied 

    # Check Redis configuration file permissions
ls -la /etc/redis/redis.conf

# Fix permissions if needed
sudo chown redis:redis /etc/redis/redis.conf
sudo chmod 640 /etc/redis/redis.conf

docs.title: Error Messages
docs.text: Error Cause Solution NOAUTH Authentication required No password provided Use -a password option ERR invalid password Wrong password Check password in .env file Could not connect to Redis Redis not running Start Redis service Connection refused Wrong host/port Check connection parameters

docs.title: Security Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Redis password configured and strong (32+ characters)
  • [ ] Password stored in environment variables
  • [ ] Redis bound to localhost only
  • [ ] Protected mode enabled
  • [ ] Dangerous commands disabled or renamed
  • [ ] Firewall rules configured
  • [ ] Regular password rotation scheduled
  • [ ] Backup encryption configured
  • [ ] Monitoring and alerting set up
  • [ ] Security testing performed

docs.title: Monthly Password Rotation
docs.text:
1. Generate New Password 

    NEW_PASSWORD=$(openssl rand -base64 32 | tr -d "=+/" | cut -c1-32)

  2. Update Configuration 

    sudo ./scripts/setup-redis-secure.sh --password "$NEW_PASSWORD"

  3. Update Applications 

    # Update .env file
sed -i "s/REDIS_PASSWORD=.*/REDIS_PASSWORD=$NEW_PASSWORD/" .env

# Restart applications
pm2 restart all

  4. Verify Connectivity 

    redis-cli -a "$NEW_PASSWORD" ping

docs.title: Security Audit Log
docs.text:
Keep a record of security changes:

# Log password rotation
echo "$(date): Redis password rotated by $(whoami)" >> /var/log/redis-security.log

# Log configuration changes
echo "$(date): Redis configuration updated by $(whoami)" >> /var/log/redis-security.log

docs.title: Compliance Requirements
docs.text:
• PCI DSS: If handling payment data, ensure Redis meets PCI requirements
  • SOX: For financial systems, maintain audit trails
  • GDPR: If storing personal data, ensure proper encryption and access controls

docs.title: Password Reset
docs.text:
If Redis password is lost or compromised:

  1. Stop Redis 

    sudo systemctl stop redis

  2. Temporarily Disable Auth 

    sudo sed -i 's/requirepass/#requirepass/' /etc/redis/redis.conf

  3. Start Redis 

    sudo systemctl start redis

  4. Set New Password 

    redis-cli CONFIG SET requirepass "new_secure_password"

  5. Update Configuration 

    sudo ./scripts/setup-redis-secure.sh --password "new_secure_password"

docs.title: Security Incident Response
docs.text:
1. Immediate Actions
  2. Change Redis password immediately
  3. Check Redis logs for unauthorized access
  4. Review application logs for anomalies

  5. Notify security team

  6. Investigation

  7. Analyze Redis command history
  8. Check network connections
  9. Review system access logs

  10. Document findings

  11. Recovery

  12. Restore from clean backup if needed
  13. Update security configurations
  14. Implement additional monitoring
  15. Update incident response procedures
